title: AI agents found vulns in this popular Linux and Unix print server - theregister.com
summary:
Here are three bullet points summarizing the news article:

• Researchers have discovered two vulnerabilities in the popular Linux and Unix print server, CUPS (Common Unix Printing System), which can be chained together to allow an unauthenticated attacker to remotely execute code and achieve root file overwrite on a network.

• The first vulnerability, CVE-2026-34980, requires the CUPS server to be reachable over the network and exposed, allowing an attacker to submit a print job that can lead to remote code execution as lp. The second vulnerability, CVE-2026-34990, is an authorization flaw that allows a local, unprivileged user to trick the CUPS scheduler daemon into authenticating to an attacker-controlled localhost IPP service.

• The vulnerabilities were discovered by a team of security researchers and have not yet been patched, although there are public commits with fixes available. The discovery highlights the challenge of keeping up with the rapid pace of code changes and vulnerability discoveries, as AI-powered bug-hunting agents continue to improve their ability to identify vulnerabilities but human maintainers struggle to keep pace.

title: SpaceX Just Gave Bad News to AT&T, Verizon, and T-Mobile Investors - The Motley Fool
summary:
Here are three bullet points summarizing the news article:

• SpaceX is planning to raise up to $75 billion through its upcoming initial public offering (IPO) and could become a major player in the wireless industry, potentially disrupting the business of AT&T, Verizon, and T-Mobile.

• The company has applied to participate in the Federal Communications Commission's AWS-3 wireless spectrum auction, which could drive up prices for wireless spectrum licenses used by the three main U.S. carriers, further increasing costs for them.

• While SpaceX may not significantly impact the current market, its long-term ambitions could make it a competitor to AT&T, Verizon, and T-Mobile in the wireless industry, potentially leading to increased competition and pressure on these companies to adapt and innovate.

title: XAI Reorganizes Its Engineering Team Ahead of SpaceX IPO - Business Insider
summary:
Here are three bullet points summarizing the news article:

• SpaceX is undergoing another major overhaul of its AI company xAI, which has been in flux since its acquisition by SpaceX in February. The company is reorganizing its engineering team and leadership roles ahead of an expected IPO that could value the company in trillions.

• The new organizational structure includes several key hires, including Devendra Chaplot leading model training, Aman Madaan overseeing model factory and tooling, Aditya Gupta heading post-training and reinforcement learning, and Beibin Li leading post-training for Grok Code. Other leadership roles have been taken on by SpaceX employees.

• Despite the reorganization, xAI is still facing challenges, including a "embarrassingly low" training performance on its compute infrastructure. The company plans to improve this significantly in the next two months as it seeks to catch up with AI rivals and prepare for an IPO.

title: macOS app Little Snitch is now available on Linux - OMG! Ubuntu
summary:
Here are three bullet points summarizing the news article:

• A Linux version of Little Snitch, a popular network monitoring tool for macOS, has been released by Christian Starkjohann of Objective Development. The new tool uses eBPF for kernel-level traffic interception and is written in Rust.

• Unlike its macOS counterpart, the Linux version of Little Snitch focuses on privacy rather than security due to resource limits imposed by eBPF, which can make it harder to reliably detect malicious activity. It provides a web-based interface for monitoring network connections and blocking suspicious processes.

• The Linux version of Little Snitch is available now and runs on distributions with Linux kernel 6.12 or above and built with BTF support (e.g., Ubuntu 25.04 or newer). While the tool itself is free to download and use, its backend portion is not open source due to "twenty years of experience" that Christian Starkjohann wants to keep closed for now.
